One of the things that we've tried to talk about is, as a firm, what should we do, and what should our manifesto or our true north commitment be? I will say that I think Mavron's benefited from having diversity and females around our investment table. We've got the best. I feel like I can go to my partners and ask for help and understanding on investments better now than ever because we've got more diversity now than we ever had. And, you know, we're also investing in consumer businesses. So it always was interesting to me how many partnerships you're investing in a business that's 80% women. And many partnerships don't have anyone sitting at the table. And I think, you know, some of the best VCs have now kind of realized that that's not a good equilibrium. So directionally, I think we're better off. Where does this all end up? I don't know. But I think the most interesting thing that's happened is we've gone from, well, first of all, I would say, how could you not admire the women who came forward, particularly the ones who spoke publicly? I admire that a lot. And that's courageous. I think we've also begun to get into a culture now where if you know it's a problem and you've tolerated it, then by definition, you're guilty. And I think that's probably healthy. So, but it's interesting to watch it in the entrepreneur world also because we've gone from Travis is the ideal entrepreneur to Travis is the horror of an entrepreneur very quick. And, you know, I think, I would say that I think we need people who are disruptive and willing to break a lot of glass to create great companies. Somehow, though, they need to kind of have a modulation. And for some people, that's hard. Yeah, that's not easy. That's not easy. Yeah, that's something that actually there was more than one person in this room. So, you know, I'd love to hear, like, what the thoughts are around that. So thanks for sharing that because that is just an interesting topic. So, you know, I was a board member of a public company. And we had an African-American CEO, and he wanted more diversity on the board. And while I was lead director, we recruited a number of people, three new people, no, four new people. And two of them were African-American females. They brought a really good perspective to that board that the rest of us needed to hear. And, you know, you see that time and time again. Yeah, no, it's glad now that it's actually, the white elephant in the room is actually being talked about, which is great to see. So we've got about 10, 12, 15 minutes. So open it up for questions. Do you have a question? I have a question about the lifetime of a VP firm kind of makes the time to an end that kind of artificially short in the company. So you were talking about building a company, the volunteer sector, and that's the 10-year lifetime of a fund. Sure. So let me read the question. So he's asking about the life of a fund. There is some, actually, some milestones that happen within that fund. Well, every, or typical VC fund is a 10-year fund. The first two to five years you make new commitments, and it's a 10-year fund with three one-year extensions. That's the way Maveron works. What I would say is, by year six, seven, or eight, we typically know, do we think this company, by year two or three or four, we typically know, does it have what we call VC optionality? By year seven or eight or nine, we kind of have a pretty good idea of what we think is the likely outcome. Sometimes we're wrong. But I think that, I'm not sure I'm a buyer, guyer, of that it's the VC time constraint. It's not the fund lifetime that forces people to be, to push entrepreneurs to move faster. It's the hits nature of the business and the need to put up numbers. So, you know, you hit that J-curve in the first year or two, and the best firms are hoping that they don't. And I think it's more the impulsiveness of trying to manage returns than it is the 10-year problem. So, Trupanion, we still own two-thirds of our position in Trupanion. It's in a 2004 fund, and our institutional investors said, Dan, you've been on the board 10 years. Whatever you think, let us know. And when you think it's time to distribute, great, and there's no rush. Now, you know, that's one that's worked. But I don't think it's the lifetime of the fund that's the problem, is, I guess, my point. And most funds are now extending, now. More than, what, one year up? Oh, yeah. You know, I mean, we have one company left in our 2,000 fund. Now, the LPs say, we explain, what do you want us to do? You know, and they say, keep managing it, and, you know, let's try and get out. And we say, we're trying. Yeah. Go ahead. The first question, did you have an opportunity to invest in Uber? You mentioned it earlier. We, anyone had an opportunity to invest in Uber because it was on AngelList. Oh. But no, we didn't, we didn't see it. Okay. And the other question is, you said that in many given years, there's about 1,500 companies that's a consumer business like you could invest in, and there's probably only five good ones. How do you go about ensuring that you have a fair shot at evaluating all of them? I hear VCs say that they only listen to people that say in buyout and production. So how do you cover those 15-year opportunities? Do you see people are now doing this in production? I'm just wondering what the results are of that. Well, I think you try to put yourself in the position where if you've built a brand that people want, hopefully, A, the vast majority of those 1,500 or whatever the number is are going to come to you. B, the I only want to talk to references is partly a screen to see how resourceful the entrepreneur is. Once I invest in you, I want to make sure that you can get to people, that you can recruit people, that you can find the best in breed companies in that sector and get to know them. So if you can't figure out a way to get to me, you're kind of, you've probably failed the test. And I know that sometimes that's hard, but in this world of LinkedIn and everything else, going to the partners and going to LinkedIn and trying to find someone. Now, you know, is there a one in a 10,000 where somebody hasn't been around and is brand new? Perhaps. But I would say that in general, like, for instance, I'm always impressed by how our best companies are finding people, and hopefully we're introducing many of them, but on their own, they're finding people as resources to help their company. They're finding people who have been there, who have done that before them. And so I think that's just another test. Yeah. And, you know, just a couple of things there, because this question gets asked me a lot just because I know a lot of people, right? So every VC does this, every one of them. If you can't get to them via some human being other than them, then you are failing, because everyone can do that. There's way too many people in this world, and it's just a little work. So that is the wrong thing to think. Like, you can always get to some other human being by going to these events, you know, LinkedIn people. I mean, that should be just the first step of the process. And every VC I've ever met, that's what they use it for. It's a filter. Particularly the world we live in, which is so connected. Yeah. 100% we do outbound. 100%. I think once you start waiting for what comes, by definition, you're complacent. You know, candidly, every person has a slightly different orientation of how they do outbound. Some are much more analytical. Some are much more networking and people. But, you know, the 20 and something, 20 and 30 something people that we have in our firm, they're meeting five to eight companies a day. And some of that is inbound and a lot of it is outbound. There's all sorts of data sources that are out there now that shows when companies start getting credit card traction and things like that. So we do some of that. And, you know, you build a network as a VC of people that you've invested with before and who are hopefully out there sourcing for you. Yeah, absolutely. Go ahead. Thank you. So I've got a question for you. So back in 1993, when Starbucks went public. 92. 92. You asked Howard Schultz, you know, you're having fun and I'm not. What's up? Right. You said, well, you don't put yourself in a position to win. So that was interesting. When I read that, it got me thinking. So first question, are you having fun? Absolutely. More than ever, thankfully. What did that mean to you first? Second of all, what did you do to put yourself in a position to win? And then what do you define when you did? Sure. Well, I think the first question that Howard forced me to, and I don't know, how many people have heard that story? Can you fill up the background? Yeah, sure. The background of the story is I was sitting with Howard in 1993, a year after the company went public. And it was still a super tiny company, $400 million market cap. And I said to Howard, how come you're having all the fun? You seem to be loving this. I'm traveling. I'm an investment banker. On the one hand, I'm getting paid a lot more than you. But on the other hand, I didn't feel like I was happy. And he said, well, you haven't put yourself in a position to win. And there were two implications of that. Really understanding, not for him, but for me, what winning was. And I think this is a thing in life that, frankly, I try to get my kids to think about a lot. I have a 15 and a 17-year-old. What's winning for you? Not winning for anyone else, but really forcing yourself to ask, what do you do well? Not what do you wish you did well? And so I think getting in touch with what you do well and what you like and what winning would be is the first step. And then the second... This was before you joined Starbucks or after you joined... I never joined Starbucks. This was while I was an investment banker. Okay. But here I am working with Starbucks, and they're a product company. They sell a physical product. And I was really impressed by the way they treated their people. And I was in an investment banking firm. And in the continuum of investment banking firms, I was in a good one. But I was not impressed when I compared it to Starbucks. So I came to understand that part of winning for me was having that psychology of treating people in a different way. So it opened my mind up to how do I find that. And I think in terms of how do you put yourself in a position to win, you have to take chance and you have to take risks. And Howard's whole thing to me when he told me the story was, you know, his father-in-law said to him, Howard, you've been at this for a few years. It's really not working. It's super hard to raise money. You know, why are you doing this? And kind of his father-in-law kind of gave him the impression that he was taking his daughter down the wrong path. And, you know, Howard just kind of realized that it was a risky thing. But if it worked, and no one has certainty that any of these things work, if it worked, he would, in his mind, win. So I think risk is harder to make bold moves the later on in life you are. I've observed that. I've had tens of investment bankers, maybe, no, I'd say tens, of 40 to 50-year-old investment bankers say, you know, I hate this, but I don't have the courage to leave. Help me. And I'm like, you know, you've got to do it yourself. But I think you don't want to get to a point where you're 40 or 50 years old, and you're either living someone else's life, or you're not doing the combination of what you love and what you do well. So I think it tends to be easier to take risks earlier. And, you know, as an MBA, I was taught not to take risks. Right? I went to HBS, and they try to tell you to analyze everything. And, you know, would the HBS framework have led me at 40 years old to quit my job that was pretty nice in terms of W-2 income and move across the country with no clarity on what I was going to do at age 40? No. But I kind of had that gut that said I was going to take a shot. So, you know, I frequently talk about a poem called Man in the Glass, which is a poem that was written about 100 years ago. And it basically says that there's only one person that you have to be true to when you're later in life, and that's the person you're looking at in the mirror. And make sure you don't cheat that person. Because if you've cheated that person, you really hurt yourself. Go ahead. I was just kind of wondering, do you think that the hashing aspect of being a form is important, but at the same time, it sounds like you said that the ability to pivot is also important, because it kind of seems like those might have a trade-off. Is there, if so, is there any way of noticing the floor itself or what I'm trying to do with one or is this together? The way I try to help people think about this is, 100%, I think, great brands are built by a combination of heart and brain. And if you're starting a consumer business now, you should architect it from the very beginning to measure everything. And you should have 100% passion about the choices you pick up front. But you need to test and learn. And if you're six months in and your hypothesis is, I should have sold a lot of this and I'm not selling it, then you have to look at the data. And you have to ask yourself why. And you have to be able to pivot. And I think the best entrepreneurs are completely passionate, but they're open to being kind of proven wrong. And so I think it's a data speaks kind of thing. It doesn't have to speak in the first week or the first month. But most of the time when we see businesses that aren't working, you know, over a six to 12-month period, okay, the data speaks. Now what are we going to do to change? And re-ignite that passion when you've tweaked it? Does that answer the question? Kind of, I mean, I mean, how much data is? Does it come back to the important numbers or is it based on analytics and kind of forecasts? Well, I think it should be both. You know, when you back somebody, it's like, okay, what are you going to do in the next year? And if you do a tenth of that, why? And just believing that you're going to make a small tweak. I have a situation now with one of our companies where love the team, love the market, couldn't be more pleased with the results, but we've had a hard time selling it. And we just all need to step back and say, we came in thinking it was this, and we've got to be open-minded and broaden our opportunities to test how the hell we're going to sell it. And that includes changing the product, it includes changing the price point, and it includes changing the channels that we're selling it through. And I think part of a good venture capitalist is to thoughtfully work with a team to ask questions. Ultimately, it's the team that typically needs to find the right strategy, but our job is to be thought-provoking, supportive, but being willing to say, you know, hey, we've been looking at this, we've been looking at this for X, and here's what we got. Does this make sense? One more question, guys. Come back. What's your strategy in using social media platforms like Instagram to YouTube for your YouTube? So that's a very good question, and candidly, we're spending a bunch of time talking about that now. Like how to use it? You know, we live in a world now where you can create your brand in ways that are different than ever before. And we vacillate between letting our brand speak for itself in terms of the help we give to entrepreneurs and the results of the company, and then feeling like it's our job to be more visible. So I think we are looking at what a lot of our competitors are doing and trying to figure out what is our differentiation. There's a great book called Different, which was written by a woman named Youngmi Moon, who's one of our advisors, HBS prof, and she talks about brands, and she talks about the willingness of companies to pick a point of differentiation and really trumpet it. And three years ago when we came up with consumer-only VC as a tagline, one of the concerns some of the people around our table had was what are we giving up, you know, opportunistically? Are we going to lose stuff, and I think the answer to that is with high certainty we're going to get different deal flow. But if our job is to find 1,500 companies a year, we're going to evaluate the 1,500 relevant companies better. And if we communicate to CEOs that all we do is early-stage consumer, we're going to get a better view of that. So I think social media is an extension of articulating and penetrating that brand presence. And, you know, I mean, some of the best firms who have multiple exits don't really need to do that because they can use earned media to do that. Yeah, but you guys have been by, I don't know if it's by strategy, but you guys have been fairly quiet. I mean, you don't necessarily tell anyone. Like, yeah, you just, right. I mean, just, I would say that if we look on the outside. I don't, I don't hear, like, like, you just don't, like, people say who's Maveron and you're like, really? Well, you know, I'll think about that. Last question. No, no, no, no. Here we go. One question, we're done. I'm very interested in finding out how you view the subject matter expertise in the consumer space, in a CEO, as you know, you know, in cloud companies, enterprise and all that, you know, you have to have the pedigree and all that other stuff, right? But in consumer, how do you, how do you, how do you kind of really measure whether that CEO got it or not? I mean, I would think that it would be less relevant, but I would like to know what were those, either instinctive or actually, you know, factual or material criteria that you would view, sorry, consider to sort of, you know, evaluate the CEO as well as the other team. I've changed as I've been doing this for 19 years. I've been doing this for 19 years, my thoughts on this have kind of evolved, and I would say the most disruptive CEO's typically aren't clouded by past experience. The shoe guys at all birds, one was a professional soccer player from New Zealand, and the other was a business development guy in sustainable materials. What they have done is recruit a level below them of tremendous pattern recognition. So they just recruited the guy who ran the supply chain for Under Armour. So your seven years at Under Armour, you saw a lot in the growth of that company, and you come into all birds, which is 16 months old. The amount that you can help that team, astronomical, and, you know, we haven't really talked about how great a recruiter is the founding team, but I was super impressed whether or not it's board members or company C-level guys and gals that this team can recruit. So I would say that's one side of the coin. On the other side, you know, one of our most successful in terms of dollar-created company was Zulily, and here's two entrepreneurs who did Blue Nile, understood everything there was to understand about e-commerce, and kind of took what they had learned at Blue Nile and repositioned it against, you know, a new business model of flash sales for moms and kids. And in that particular case, that worked out very well. So I think it's, it's not a black or white question. If they have industry experience, it's, are they always talking about the rules in the industry or are they trying to break the rules? And if they don't have it, it's, can they recruit people who can really inform the team how things are typically done, but rethink it. There's a great Daryl Cabin story that I like to tell on Zulily. Daryl's working out of our office and he comes up with this, we had introduced them to a company called One King's Lane and we decided, they decided that that wasn't a fit. And so Daryl decides he's going to take a look at a, at a flash sale, different vertical, and he comes upon kids. And so he's working his way through the economics and he starts talking to photographers. And I don't know how many of you are familiar with the Zulily model, but they take hundreds of events a day. And so the cost to photograph the merchandise is a huge element of the cost to deliver the service. And Daryl talks to professional photographers who told them, you know, you can't shoot it for under, I forget what the number was, but it was a super high number. And Daryl knew that the business model didn't work if it was that he ended up doing it for a tenth of what the industry insiders told them was the cost. And that's just taking a fresh approach and being disruptive. So here he is in e-commerce, which is a category he knows, but really being disruptive. And I think it's that mix and match that is critical. Awesome. Well, thank you guys for being here.

Dude, I'd like to welcome Dan Levitan from Averon.
Come on up, Dan.
Here you go, my friend.
I feel like Russell.
Yes.
Okay.

Y'all?

Yeah.

Well, thank you for coming to spend some time today with us.
I appreciate that.
It's always nice to see you.
Smile and face.
With pleasure.
Yes.
So, let's give your, kind of give your elevator pitch for Maveron so everyone clearly knows what you guys do over there.
I mean, I can tell them that you can do a better job.
Sure.
The elevator pitch for Maveron is, for the last 19 years, we've been a consumer-only venture capital firm.
And I was talking with Pam earlier.

I think what's happened in the last few decades is that the phone has all integrated into consumers' lives and created businesses that never really had the capability to one-to-one directly connect with consumers.
And so, we think that the world didn't need another venture capital firm that's undifferentiated and just says they're opportunistic.
So, we are a consumer-only venture firm and we're early stage, which means we do seeds and series A.
And because we're a consumer, we look, we have offices in San Francisco and Seattle, and I would say that probably New York and L.A. are the fourth, the third and fourth markets that we focus on.
And in the last, like, what, say, let's say three years, what have been some of the, I guess, successes?
Like, what would you, off the top of your head?
Well, here in town, our biggest success in the last few years was we were fortunate enough to have Zulily start in our office by Daryl and Mark.
And, you know, we've had an overnight 10-year success in True Panyan, which is a medical insurance for cats and dogs companies, which is now approaching $700 million in market cap.
But it's taken a long time.
It's a long road to get there.
400 employees down in Georgetown, but kind of a consistent grower.
And frankly, it's reframed.
I think there's a lot of glory to the go big fast.
And I meet a lot of entrepreneurs, and some of their ideas are not going to blow up and be perfect overnight for venture capitalists.
And I think they do themselves and their investors a disservice if they try to turn the company into something that it isn't or shouldn't be.
And I've seen lots of companies get screwed up by the wrong investors.
And so I think, you know, one of the things that everyone really doesn't talk about a lot in my mind in the startup world is the glory is, well, you know, how much money have you raised?
I don't really think that that's the way most businesses should be judged.
Because what if you don't ever make money and you've raised, you know, $50 million?
By the way, it's the same way for venture capital firms.
Everyone talks about how much money have you raised, what's the size of your last fund?
And very few people are really consistently disclosing returns because it's a damn hard business.
Exactly.
That sounds easy.
It's easier to raise the money than it is to replicate or remake it.
Let's talk about that, I guess, the difference between the company that should be funded by a Maveron and the company that shouldn't even go there, right?
Like, what's the difference in there?
I mean, there's people in this room.
How many people have started a company, like, in the last 12 months and are either starting a company in the next six months?
Like, so we've got, you know, a dozen, maybe 15 of those people sitting in this room, right?
So how do we talk to those 15 people and say, you know what?
You're not a VC.
Don't try to kill yourself.
That's not your model.
Like, how do we know that?
How do they?
What's a good idea?
Don't kill yourself.
Your startup can't succeed if you're dead.
Well, there's a few people, actually, in this audience who have come to talk to me.
First, the notion that I would dispel is that it's black or white.
It's either VC or it isn't.
I think in a lot of cases, it should start out, perhaps, without a lot of funding and test and learn and see where there are inflection points.
Trupanion started out as a guy with a dream in Canada on an island called Bowen Island outside of Vancouver.
And for seven years, he just raised friends and family money.
And we were the, in 2007, we were his first institutional round.
But he had gone from zero pets to 6,000 pets, insured.
So I think the first thing is, I think you need the appropriate capital strategy at the time for the idea and you and the team, as opposed to over-capitalizing it.
But that said, I think the kinds of things that are VC-oriented.
I mean, does everyone in the room kind of understand the economics of why VCs push for super big ideas?
Well, so in a private equity business, you're going to, a private equity fund, you raise a couple hundred million bucks and, you know, you hope you don't have any write-offs.
But all your returns are kind of between, say, you get half your money back and, say, a 5x.
And if you can do that, that blends out to an IRR that hopefully is between 10% and 20% and gets above the 8% hurdle for them to get carry.
And they're happy.
Venture is not like that.
Venture is a much more wild ride.
In our first fund, which was started in 1998, eBay returned the fund.
One investment.
And we had one other thing that was successful.
And then we had a bunch of things because 2,000 rolled around in 2001 that went to zero.
So in our best funds, there's one or two businesses that have outperformed everything else combined.

So in our fourth fund, you know, Zulily was, you know, I mean, when you're lucky enough to partner with great entrepreneurs and you can get in at a single-digit pre-money and it becomes a multi-billion-dollar business, you look a lot smarter than you really are.
Exactly.
Thankfully.
But so the VCs are looking for those kind of investments.
And most investments don't fit into that.
Yeah, they don't have the potential to be a billion dollars or even a half a billion dollars.
And our investors and ourselves are willing to lose money completely and move on, whereas in the private equity world, they don't really like to do that.
And I will say that's been an acquired taste for me.
I kind of started in the business not readily kind of writing it off.
Willing to write it off.
And not willing to say, you know, maybe we shouldn't participate in this big because we haven't found product-market fit.
There's been a lot of turnover in the team.
Maybe the thesis wasn't right.

And so I think a good venture capitalist is aligned up front with the team and the founders of the company and then helps the team and the founders of the company interpret the data that gives you information about was the original thesis right or not.
So many times I'm two or three years into something and the original thesis is imprinted on the entrepreneur's mind.
And I appreciate that, but change happens.
And the sooner we kind of say, well, we thought this but that, the better off we are and we can make a better decision.
Yeah.
So I think one of the things that is really important there is the fact that it's okay to change your mind.
It's totally okay.
And actually, how many out of 100 companies, how many do you think that when they started out, their idea is that's what they ended up?
How many of the 100 actually happened like that?
You're just a guess.
Many of our biggest successes started and then kind of went the way they thought.
I think the Googles of the world are rare.
You know, I've pivoted and lo and behold, it gets to be.
But I would say every business has lots of bumps in the road.
And, you know, one of the questions really is, do you have the grit and the determination to get through it?
And the scary thing is, frankly, for those of you who have been in this game since 2010, this is not going to be the environment forever.
If you're starting a company today, it's most likely that you're going to face an environment that is radically different than what it's been like from 2010 to 2017.
I can't tell you when, and I'm not a doomsday guy, but these markets go in cycles.
And, you know, we started in 98.
Everyone thought we were brilliant investing in consumer companies.
Not in 2000.
2000, 2001, they thought we were complete idiots.
Then, in 2007, we were back to brilliant.
And in 2009, everyone was shutting down consumer funds.
But, interestingly enough, 2009, a company like Zulalie walks into our office, doesn't talk to any other venture firm, and great things happen.
And, you know, many, many, many, many significant companies were created in 2009, 10, and 11.
Now, why is that?
Because those were the most determined entrepreneurs.
Those were the grittiest.
Those were the most disciplined on spending.
And I think there's a lot of lessons to be learned.
And for those of you who are starting a company, I would say, regardless of how much money is in your bank account, run the business in a way that is different than that.
Last night, Mike was saying he was on a red eye.
I was out drinking with one of my portfolio companies.
So, we had this event in L.A., and after it, we went out drinking.
And then, these guys are going back to their hotel.
And, you know, the CEO says to me, I said, where are you staying?
And they gave me the name of some L.A. hotel that I had never heard of.
And they said, you know, well, we tell our head of admin in our company that $150 maximum per night.
The company is very successful.
But the CEO, two CEOs, how they act and the message that they send permeates the company.
And, you know, I mean, I think Jeff Bezos lives that today, one of the most successful companies in the world, and partly because of that level of frugality.
That's why he keeps referring to the doors, you know, and day one and all that stuff.
And so, for those of you, I think a lot of entrepreneurs confuse initial success with changing that attitude.
And I think that's unhealthy.
And where do you think, like, in the current environment of where we are today, with just the funding environment,
where do you think that, if we look back in the last 15, I guess the 17, so look back in the last 20 years,
do you think it compares any of those years that we can, like, say, you know it's similar to X a year?
Do you think it's similar in any historical stuff we've already been through?
Let's say the last 20 years, because 20 years, most of these people were wondering around.
Well, 98, 99 was such a go-go.
And 01, 02 was so down.
I mean, if there was a time, I'd say it's 06, 07.
But I don't think we have the mortgage problem.
We'll talk about that later.
Would you disagree?
Well, I just, yeah, I think 2006, 2007, it was pretty frothy.
I mean, it was, like, the housing market was...
We have 165 multi-billion dollar valued private companies.
That is not a sustainable equilibrium.
It isn't.
You're right.
